runs.interpretation: Secondary-earner substitution-response elasticity used in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Heim (2007) on secondary earner labor supply elasticities
Keane and Rogerson (2012) labor supply survey
Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) on wage elasticity of labor supply
runs.reasoningSummary: Secondary earners (typically secondary earners in couples) exhibit higher labor supply elasticity than primary earners due to greater flexibility in work decisions. Empirical evidence suggests elasticities typically range 0.2-0.6, with secondary earners clustering toward the higher end. Tax-benefit simulations often use elasticities in the 0.3-0.5 range. I estimate p50 at 0.40 as a reasonable central estimate reflecting moderate responsiveness to wage changes, with considerable uncertainty reflected in the 0.15-0.85 range spanning both conservative and higher estimates found in the literature.

Empirical evidence suggests elasticities typically range 0.2-0.6, with secondary earners clustering toward the higher end. Tax-benefit simulations often use elasticities in the 0.3-0.5 range. I estimate p50 at 0.40 as a reasonable central estimate reflecting moderate responsiveness to wage changes, with considerable uncertainty reflected in the 0.15-0.85 range spanning both conservative and higher estimates found in the literature."}
